item.dc:title: Facile Access to N‐Substituted Pyridyl Ligands

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in Enantioselective C‐H Functionalization via Electrochemical/Transition‐Metal Cooperative Catalysis
item.dc:description:
This review summarizes recent advances in asymmetric C‐H functionalization via electrochemical/transition metal cooperative catalysis. By replacing chemical oxidants with electricity, this green strategy enables efficient redox cycling of metals (palladium, rhodium, nickel, copper, and cobalt) for enantioselective C‐H functionalization. Key mechanisms and chiral induction models for constructing central, planar, axial, and inherent chirality are highlighted.
Enantioselective C‐H functionalization has emerged as a key strategy for constructing chiral molecules, as it enables direct and precise chiral modification of inert C‐H bonds while significantly improving atom economy in synthetic processes. Traditional asymmetric C‐H functionalization relies on chemical oxidants to drive catalytic cycles, which suffers from limitations such as high toxicity of reagents, excessive byproduct formation, and harsh reaction conditions. Electrochemical/transition metal cooperative catalysis offers an innovative pathway by replacing chemical oxidants with electrical energy, utilizing electrode reactions to precisely regulate the valence cycle of metal catalysts. By integrating the C‐H bond activation capability and chiral induction ability of transition metals, this approach provides a green and efficient route for constructing chiral molecules. This review systematically summarizes the research progress over the past 5 years in electrochemical/transition metal cooperative catalysis for asymmetric C‐H functionalization toward chiral compounds. It highlights the cooperative mechanisms between different transition metals (palladium, rhodium, nickel, copper, cobalt) and electrochemistry. We believe this review will serve as an important reference for scientists interested in advances in asymmetric C‐H functionalization via electrochemical/metal cooperative catalysis.
item.content:encoded:
This review summarizes recent advances in asymmetric C-H functionalization via electrochemical/transition metal cooperative catalysis. By replacing chemical oxidants with electricity, this green strategy enables efficient redox cycling of metals (palladium, rhodium, nickel, copper, and cobalt) for enantioselective C-H functionalization. Key mechanisms and chiral induction models for constructing central, planar, axial, and inherent chirality are highlighted.


Enantioselective C-H functionalization has emerged as a key strategy for constructing chiral molecules, as it enables direct and precise chiral modification of inert C-H bonds while significantly improving atom economy in synthetic processes. Traditional asymmetric C-H functionalization relies on chemical oxidants to drive catalytic cycles, which suffers from limitations such as high toxicity of reagents, excessive byproduct formation, and harsh reaction conditions. Electrochemical/transition metal cooperative catalysis offers an innovative pathway by replacing chemical oxidants with electrical energy, utilizing electrode reactions to precisely regulate the valence cycle of metal catalysts. By integrating the C-H bond activation capability and chiral induction ability of transition metals, this approach provides a green and efficient route for constructing chiral molecules. This review systematically summarizes the research progress over the past 5 years in electrochemical/transition metal cooperative catalysis for asymmetric C-H functionalization toward chiral compounds. It highlights the cooperative mechanisms between different transition metals (palladium, rhodium, nickel, copper, cobalt) and electrochemistry. We believe this review will serve as an important reference for scientists interested in advances in asymmetric C-H functionalization via electrochemical/metal cooperative catalysis.

item.dc:title: Recent Advances in Enantioselective C‐H Functionalization via Electrochemical/Transition‐Metal Cooperative Catalysis

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper‐Mediated ortho‐C–H Etherification of Arylaldehydes Enabled by a Transient Directing Group
item.dc:description:
A copper‐mediated ortho‐C–H activation of aromatic aldehydes coupled with dehydroetherification of alcohols was successfully developed using a transient directing group strategy. This reaction signifies the inaugural etherification of phenolic compounds under transient directing group conditions, underscoring the promise of cost‐effective copper catalysts for challenging C–O bond formation and propelling sustainable C–H functionalization.
Transient directing groups (TDGs) have been demonstrated to promote the in situ generation and elimination of directing groups, thereby providing an efficient strategy for C–H functionalization. This study utilizes the in situ formation of imines between amino acids and aromatic aldehydes. Under copper‐mediated conditions, an ortho‐C–H etherification reaction between aromatic aldehydes and phenols/alcohols was achieved. To the best of our knowledge, this represents the first example of a TDG‐mediated reaction involving phenol substrates. The utility of this strategy has been demonstrated through multiple applications, including the late‐stage diversification of drug analogs.

This reaction signifies the inaugural etherification of phenolic compounds under transient directing group conditions, underscoring the promise of cost-effective copper catalysts for challenging C–O bond formation and propelling sustainable C–H functionalization.

To the best of our knowledge, this represents the first example of a TDG-mediated reaction involving phenol substrates. The utility of this strategy has been demonstrated through multiple applications, including the late-stage diversification of drug analogs.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Base‐Promoted Multicomponent Reaction of Phthalaldehyde With Amines and Sulfur: Access to 3‐Iminoisoindoline‐1‐Thiones
item.dc:description:
A base‐catalyzed, oxidative multicomponent condensation involving primary anilines, elemental sulfur, and phthalaldehyde is reported. Utilizing readily available starting materials, this protocol affords a novel series of structurally diverse 3‐iminoisoindoline‐1‐thiones in satisfactory yields under mild reaction conditions.
A base‐catalyzed, oxidative multicomponent condensation involving primary anilines, elemental sulfur, and phthalaldehyde is reported. Utilizing readily available starting materials, this protocol affords a novel series of structurally diverse 3‐iminoisoindoline‐1‐thiones in satisfactory yields under mild reaction conditions. Furthermore, the methodology was successfully extended to 1,8‐diaminonaphthalene, yielding the photosensitizer 12H‐isoindolo[2,1‐a]perimidine‐12‐thione in excellent yields with a simplified purification process via filtration.

Utilizing readily available starting materials, this protocol affords a novel series of structurally diverse 3-iminoisoindoline-1-thiones in satisfactory yields under mild reaction conditions.

Furthermore, the methodology was successfully extended to 1,8-diaminonaphthalene, yielding the photosensitizer 12H-isoindolo[2,1-a]perimidine-12-thione in excellent yields with a simplified purification process via filtration.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Water‐Assisted Fe(CO)5 Catalyzed Transfer Hydrodesulfurization of Thioamides
item.dc:description:
Iron mediated selective desulfurization of thioamides to amine has been achieved in just 4 hours under the facile condition of time and temperature. Geen solvent water was used as solvent and surrogate of hydrogen. Method tolerates a range of functionally diverse thioamides and found applicable for synthesis of dideuterated amines in significant amount.
Herein, an efficient method for a chemo‐selective hydrodesulfurization of thioamides to secondary and tertiary amines was achieved via a selective Fe(CO)5‐catalyzed cleavage of CS bond in water. This work presents a unique and rare methodology, as it utilizes water as a surrogate of hydrogen to functionalize various thioamide derivatives. Moreover, α,α‐dideuterated amine was also prepared using inexpensive deuterium oxide (D2O). DFT calculations were performed to verify the mechanistic insights of the reaction.
item.content:encoded:
Iron mediated selective desulfurization of thioamides to amine has been achieved in just 4 hours under the facile condition of time and temperature. Geen solvent water was used as solvent and surrogate of hydrogen. Method tolerates a range of functionally diverse thioamides and found applicable for synthesis of dideuterated amines in significant amount.


Herein, an efficient method for a chemo-selective hydrodesulfurization of thioamides to secondary and tertiary amines was achieved via a selective Fe(CO)5-catalyzed cleavage of CS bond in water. This work presents a unique and rare methodology, as it utilizes water as a surrogate of hydrogen to functionalize various thioamide derivatives. Moreover, α,α-dideuterated amine was also prepared using inexpensive deuterium oxide (D2O). DFT calculations were performed to verify the mechanistic insights of the reaction.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Ce‐Incorporation‐Induced Crystal and Electronic Structure Regulation of Amorphous NiP Alloy to Boost Hydrogen Evolution in Anion Exchange Membrane Water Electrolysis
item.dc:description:
Ce incorporation into amorphous NiP alloy greatly boosts hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activity and stability, which can be attributed to appropriate OH binding energy during HER, accelerated charge and mass transfer at the catalyst/substrate interface, and negligible internal stress, outperforming the state‐of‐the‐art NiP‐based electrocatalysts.
Anion exchange membrane water electrolysis (AEMWE) driven by intermittent renewable energy is one of the optimal solutions for producing green hydrogen gas; however, its further development is limited by highly active and long‐lived hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) catalysts. Herein, a cerium (Ce) incorporated amorphous NiP alloy grown on nickel foam is proposed to meet these challenges, which shows an industrial‐level current density of −1000 mA/cm2 under 255 mV overpotential and maintains excellent stability under intermittent start‐shutdown operation for 10k cycles in 1 M KOH. The experimental results reveal that the incorporation of Ce can optimize the electronic structure and endow appropriate OH binding energy during HER, thereby significantly accelerating HER kinetics. Moreover, the optimization of NiP crystal structure induced by Ce incorporation can adjust the surface microstructure and release the interfacial stress between the catalytic layer and substrate, resulting in accelerated charge and mass transfer and enhanced stability during the HER process. When integrated into an AEMWE, the optimized NiCeP catalyst displays a low cell voltage of 1.79 V at 1000 mA/cm2 and maintains stable operation up to 200 h under industrial conditions. This work highlights the importance of modification in the crystal and electronic structure of catalysts in advancing AEMWE under fluctuating renewable energy driving conditions.
item.content:encoded:
Ce incorporation into amorphous NiP alloy greatly boosts hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activity and stability, which can be attributed to appropriate OH binding energy during HER, accelerated charge and mass transfer at the catalyst/substrate interface, and negligible internal stress, outperforming the state-of-the-art NiP-based electrocatalysts.


Anion exchange membrane water electrolysis (AEMWE) driven by intermittent renewable energy is one of the optimal solutions for producing green hydrogen gas; however, its further development is limited by highly active and long-lived hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) catalysts. Herein, a cerium (Ce) incorporated amorphous NiP alloy grown on nickel foam is proposed to meet these challenges, which shows an industrial-level current density of −1000 mA/cm2 under 255 mV overpotential and maintains excellent stability under intermittent start-shutdown operation for 10k cycles in 1 M KOH. The experimental results reveal that the incorporation of Ce can optimize the electronic structure and endow appropriate OH binding energy during HER, thereby significantly accelerating HER kinetics. Moreover, the optimization of NiP crystal structure induced by Ce incorporation can adjust the surface microstructure and release the interfacial stress between the catalytic layer and substrate, resulting in accelerated charge and mass transfer and enhanced stability during the HER process. When integrated into an AEMWE, the optimized NiCeP catalyst displays a low cell voltage of 1.79 V at 1000 mA/cm2 and maintains stable operation up to 200 h under industrial conditions. This work highlights the importance of modification in the crystal and electronic structure of catalysts in advancing AEMWE under fluctuating renewable energy driving conditions.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in Electrochemical Nucleophilic Introduction of Azide and Functionalization
item.dc:description:
Azides are significant precursors in various chemical transformations, especially for the preparation of amines, amides, nitriles, triazoles, tetrazoles, and pyrrolines. Organic electrochemistry has garnered increasing attention due to its essential potential to promote the development of sustainable organic synthesis, enabling the new avenues to introduce the azide group or directly realize the functionalization in the past decade. This review aims to highlight recent advancements in the nucleophilic introduction or functionalization of azide compounds facilitated by electrochemical protocols. The representative scopes and electrochemical mechanism are emphatically described, aiming to inspire innovations in the development of azide chemistry.
Azides, as multifunctional nitrogen‐containing molecules, are significant precursors in various chemical transformations, especially for the preparation of amines, amides, nitriles, triazoles, tetrazoles, and pyrrolines by subsequent redox, cycloaddition, click chemistry, and rearrangement processes. Organic electrochemistry has garnered increasing attention due to its essential potential to promote the development of sustainable organic synthesis, enabling the new avenues to introduce the azide group or directly realize the functionalization in the past decade. In this review, the research progress on the nucleophilic incorporation and direct functionalization of azide groups through electrochemical methodologies is critically summarized based on the electrophilic species involved. The representative scopes and electrochemical mechanism are emphatically described, aiming to inspire innovations in the development of azide chemistry.

The representative scopes and electrochemical mechanism are emphatically described, aiming to inspire innovations in the development of azide chemistry.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: N‐Heterocyclic Carbene‐Catalyzed Hauser–Kraus Annulation with 1,3‐Cyclopentenediones: Formal Total Synthesis of Fredericamycin A
item.dc:description:
We developed an N‐heterocyclic carbene‐catalyzed hydroquinone formation reaction. The methyl ortho‐formylbenzoate was used as a starting material, and reacted with the cyclopentene‐1,3‐diones, to afford the desired hydroquinone products in good yields. More importantly, with the help of this powerful method, the facile synthesis of Fredericamycin A was achieved in 11 steps. Several analogs of FDM‐A are readily available as well. Preliminary biological evaluation demonstrated that these analogs exhibited moderate to weak cytotoxicity against CCRF‐CEM cells.
Fredericamycin A (FDM‐A) has garnered significant interest from the synthetic community due to its fascinating chemical structure and notable antitumor activity since its first isolation in 1981. Herein, we developed an N‐heterocyclic carbene‐catalyzed hydroquinone formation reaction. The methyl ortho‐formylbenzoate was used as a starting material and reacted with the cyclopentene‐1,3‐diones to afford the desired hydroquinone products in good yields. The reaction undergoes in mild conditions at room temperature, making the reaction system well‐tolerated for many functional groups. Furthermore, the enantioselective variant of this hydroquinone formation was realized with a chiral NHC precatalyst, affording the product with moderate enantioselectivity. With the help of our method, the synthesis of FDM‐A was achieved. Moreover, several analogs of FDM‐A are readily available as well. Preliminary cytotoxicity evaluation revealed that these analogs exhibited moderate to weak inhibitory activity against CCRF‐CEM cells.
item.content:encoded:
We developed an N-heterocyclic carbene-catalyzed hydroquinone formation reaction. The methyl ortho-formylbenzoate was used as a starting material, and reacted with the cyclopentene-1,3-diones, to afford the desired hydroquinone products in good yields. More importantly, with the help of this powerful method, the facile synthesis of Fredericamycin A was achieved in 11 steps. Several analogs of FDM-A are readily available as well. Preliminary biological evaluation demonstrated that these analogs exhibited moderate to weak cytotoxicity against CCRF-CEM cells.


Fredericamycin A (FDM-A) has garnered significant interest from the synthetic community due to its fascinating chemical structure and notable antitumor activity since its first isolation in 1981. Herein, we developed an N-heterocyclic carbene-catalyzed hydroquinone formation reaction. The methyl ortho-formylbenzoate was used as a starting material and reacted with the cyclopentene-1,3-diones to afford the desired hydroquinone products in good yields. The reaction undergoes in mild conditions at room temperature, making the reaction system well-tolerated for many functional groups. Furthermore, the enantioselective variant of this hydroquinone formation was realized with a chiral NHC precatalyst, affording the product with moderate enantioselectivity. With the help of our method, the synthesis of FDM-A was achieved. Moreover, several analogs of FDM-A are readily available as well. Preliminary cytotoxicity evaluation revealed that these analogs exhibited moderate to weak inhibitory activity against CCRF-CEM cells.
item.dc:creator: Panlong Ren, Hongrong Ren
item.dc:title: N‐Heterocyclic Carbene‐Catalyzed Hauser–Kraus Annulation with 1,3‐Cyclopentenediones: Formal Total Synthesis of Fredericamycin A

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Halogen‐Atom‐Transfer Enabled Radical Fluoroalkyl‐Thiolation of Unactivated Alkenes
item.dc:description:
Here, we report an aryl radical‐induced three‐component fluoroalkyl‐thiolation of unactivated olefins through halogen atom transfer process, enabling rapid simultaneous construction of C(sp3)–Rf and C(sp3)–S bonds with high regioselectivity. Similarly, direct thiolation of fluoroalkyl iodides in absence of alkenes affords the fluoroalkylthioethers.
We develop a halogen atom transfer‐enabled radical fluoroalkyl‐thiolation of unactivated alkenes employing fluoroalkyl iodides, TMEDA‐ligated zinc thiolates. Notably, the aryldiazonium salts were utilized as the promoter in the three‐component reaction, providing a powerful tool for simultaneous construction of CRf and CS bonds across the CC bond. Moreover, direct thiolation of fluoroalkyl iodides without alkene gives the two‐component reaction products. Mechanistic studies supported that the interaction between diazonium salt and zinc thiolate plays a crucial role in the transformations.
item.content:encoded:
Here, we report an aryl radical-induced three-component fluoroalkyl-thiolation of unactivated olefins through halogen atom transfer process, enabling rapid simultaneous construction of C(sp 3)–Rf and C(sp 3)–S bonds with high regioselectivity. Similarly, direct thiolation of fluoroalkyl iodides in absence of alkenes affords the fluoroalkylthioethers.


We develop a halogen atom transfer-enabled radical fluoroalkyl-thiolation of unactivated alkenes employing fluoroalkyl iodides, TMEDA-ligated zinc thiolates. Notably, the aryldiazonium salts were utilized as the promoter in the three-component reaction, providing a powerful tool for simultaneous construction of CRf and CS bonds across the CC bond. Moreover, direct thiolation of fluoroalkyl iodides without alkene gives the two-component reaction products. Mechanistic studies supported that the interaction between diazonium salt and zinc thiolate plays a crucial role in the transformations.
item.dc:creator: Yanchuang Zhao, Yule Xie, Aman Ullah, Ying Bai, Shao‐Fei Ni, Xinxin Shao
item.dc:title: Halogen‐Atom‐Transfer Enabled Radical Fluoroalkyl‐Thiolation of Unactivated Alkenes

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in Lignin Valorization: Catalytic Strategies for the Production of Renewable Aromatics
item.dc:description:
This review summarizes recent catalytic strategies for converting lignin into renewable aromatic chemicals. By comparing stepwise and one‐step conversion strategies, it emphasizes how catalyst design and reaction pathway control enable selective depolymerization and upgrading. Key advances and remaining challenges in lignin‐to‐aromatics conversion are discussed.
Lignin valorization remains a central yet unresolved bottleneck in the sustainable production of renewable aromatic chemicals, owing to its intrinsic structural heterogeneity and the facile condensation of lignin‐derived intermediates. Over the past decade, advances in catalysis have shifted the field toward strategies that exert precise control over bond scission, intermediate stabilization, and reaction pathway selection. This review critically examines recent progress in catalytic lignin valorization by contrasting stepwise and one‐step conversion strategies. Stepwise strategies, in which depolymerization and upgrading are deliberately decoupled, effectively suppress condensation reactions and enable independent optimization of catalytic functions; within this framework, reductive catalytic fractionation followed by upgrading is discussed, highlighting how metal identity, metal‐support interactions, and acidity synergistically govern selective bond cleavage while preserving aromatic integrity. In parallel, emerging one‐step conversion strategies are evaluated as fully integrated catalytic systems that couple depolymerization and upgrading within a single reaction environment, offering opportunities for process intensification but accompanied by inherent activity‐selectivity trade‐offs. By integrating insights across catalyst design and reaction pathway engineering, this review delineates key challenges, identifies fundamental trade‐offs, and outlines future directions toward scalable lignin‐to‐aromatics manufacturing.
item.content:encoded:
This review summarizes recent catalytic strategies for converting lignin into renewable aromatic chemicals. By comparing stepwise and one-step conversion strategies, it emphasizes how catalyst design and reaction pathway control enable selective depolymerization and upgrading. Key advances and remaining challenges in lignin-to-aromatics conversion are discussed.


Lignin valorization remains a central yet unresolved bottleneck in the sustainable production of renewable aromatic chemicals, owing to its intrinsic structural heterogeneity and the facile condensation of lignin-derived intermediates. Over the past decade, advances in catalysis have shifted the field toward strategies that exert precise control over bond scission, intermediate stabilization, and reaction pathway selection. This review critically examines recent progress in catalytic lignin valorization by contrasting stepwise and one-step conversion strategies. Stepwise strategies, in which depolymerization and upgrading are deliberately decoupled, effectively suppress condensation reactions and enable independent optimization of catalytic functions; within this framework, reductive catalytic fractionation followed by upgrading is discussed, highlighting how metal identity, metal-support interactions, and acidity synergistically govern selective bond cleavage while preserving aromatic integrity. In parallel, emerging one-step conversion strategies are evaluated as fully integrated catalytic systems that couple depolymerization and upgrading within a single reaction environment, offering opportunities for process intensification but accompanied by inherent activity-selectivity trade-offs. By integrating insights across catalyst design and reaction pathway engineering, this review delineates key challenges, identifies fundamental trade-offs, and outlines future directions toward scalable lignin-to-aromatics manufacturing.
item.dc:creator: Hexuan Zhang, Jialing Dong, Prasert Reubroycharoen, Song Song, Xingang Li
item.dc:title: Recent Advances in Lignin Valorization: Catalytic Strategies for the Production of Renewable Aromatics

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Post‐Synthetic Sulfonation of Metal–Organic Frameworks for Efficient Aziridine Ring‐Opening Functionalization
item.dc:description:
Asulfonic acid–functionalized metal–organic framework catalyst enablessolvent‐free ring‐opening functionalization of aziridines with diversenucleophiles, delivering 1,2‐difunctionalized products in high yields undermild conditions with broad scope and recyclability.
A sulfonic acid–functionalized metal–organic framework catalyst (MIL‐101‐Fe‐NH‐R‐SO3H, R = CH2CH2CH2) was prepared via post‐synthetic modification through an irreversible ring‐opening reaction with 1,3‐propane sultone. Structural characterization confirmed the successful incorporation of Brønsted acidic sites while preserving the framework integrity. The resulting catalyst efficiently promotes the ring‐opening functionalization of aziridines with a wide range of nucleophiles, including alcohols, amines, water, and azide sources, under mild and solvent‐free conditions, affording diverse 1,2‐difunctionalized products in good to excellent yields. Broad substrate scope, high functional group tolerance, gram‐scale applicability, and good recyclability highlight the practicality of this protocol.
item.content:encoded:
Asulfonic acid–functionalized metal–organic framework catalyst enablessolvent-free ring-opening functionalization of aziridines with diversenucleophiles, delivering 1,2-difunctionalized products in high yields undermild conditions with broad scope and recyclability.


A sulfonic acid–functionalized metal–organic framework catalyst (MIL-101-Fe-NH-R-SO3H, R = CH2CH2CH2) was prepared via post-synthetic modification through an irreversible ring-opening reaction with 1,3-propane sultone. Structural characterization confirmed the successful incorporation of Brønsted acidic sites while preserving the framework integrity. The resulting catalyst efficiently promotes the ring-opening functionalization of aziridines with a wide range of nucleophiles, including alcohols, amines, water, and azide sources, under mild and solvent-free conditions, affording diverse 1,2-difunctionalized products in good to excellent yields. Broad substrate scope, high functional group tolerance, gram-scale applicability, and good recyclability highlight the practicality of this protocol.
item.dc:creator: Ailing Gu, Conghui Tang
item.dc:title: Post‐Synthetic Sulfonation of Metal–Organic Frameworks for Efficient Aziridine Ring‐Opening Functionalization

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Functionalized Diaryliodonium Salts with N‐Reactive Amides: Versatile Reactivity for Constructing Benzo‐Fused Nitrogen Heterocycles
item.dc:description:
A practical one‐pot strategy for the synthesis of ortho‐amide‐functionalized TMP‐iodonium(III) salts has been developed. These iodonium salts exhibited distinct reactivities toward N‐, O‐, and S‐nucleophiles, facilitating arylocyclization and producing a variety of benzo‐fused heterocycles under mild conditions.
Diaryliodonium(III) salts with N‐reactive amides are proposed as new attractive synthetic tools for the construction of benzo‐fused nitrogen heterocycles. In this study, we have developed an efficient preparation method for ortho‐functionalized diaryliodonium salts bearing N‐phthalimide (N‐NPhth) groups. Unlike strategies requiring ortho‐leaving groups, these salts demonstrated their application in both transition metal‐free and transition metal‐catalyzed sequential coupling reactions, leading to the construction of five‐ and seven‐membered heterocyclic compounds.
item.content:encoded:
A practical one-pot strategy for the synthesis of ortho-amide-functionalized TMP-iodonium(III) salts has been developed. These iodonium salts exhibited distinct reactivities toward N-, O-, and S-nucleophiles, facilitating arylocyclization and producing a variety of benzo-fused heterocycles under mild conditions.


Diaryliodonium(III) salts with N-reactive amides are proposed as new attractive synthetic tools for the construction of benzo-fused nitrogen heterocycles. In this study, we have developed an efficient preparation method for ortho-functionalized diaryliodonium salts bearing N-phthalimide (N-NPhth) groups. Unlike strategies requiring ortho-leaving groups, these salts demonstrated their application in both transition metal-free and transition metal-catalyzed sequential coupling reactions, leading to the construction of five- and seven-membered heterocyclic compounds.
item.dc:creator: Naoki Miyamoto, Kotaro Kikushima, Arisu Kasai, Yasuyuki Kita, Toshifumi Dohi
item.dc:title: Functionalized Diaryliodonium Salts with N‐Reactive Amides: Versatile Reactivity for Constructing Benzo‐Fused Nitrogen Heterocycles

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Metal‐Free Synthesis of Indolo[3,2‐c]quinolines via S8‐Mediated Hydrogen‐Borrowing Reduction/Cyclization/Aromatization Sequence
item.dc:description:
A S8‐mediated hydrogen‐borrowing reduction/cyclization/aromatization strategy for the preparation of indolo[3,2‐c]quinolines has been described. In this work, nitro‐group reduction, CN bond cleavage, CN bond formation, and cyclization are achieved in one pot.
A S8‐mediated hydrogen‐borrowing reduction/cyclization/aromatization strategy for the preparation of indolo[3,2‐c]quinolines has been described. The present procedure starts from 2‐(2‐nitrophenyl)indoles and amines, providing a wide range of indolo[3,2‐c]quinolines in 39%–75% yields under metal‐free conditions. In this work, nitro‐group reduction, CN bond cleavage, CN bond formation, and cyclization are achieved in one pot.
item.content:encoded:
A S8-mediated hydrogen-borrowing reduction/cyclization/aromatization strategy for the preparation of indolo[3,2-c]quinolines has been described. In this work, nitro-group reduction, CN bond cleavage, CN bond formation, and cyclization are achieved in one pot.


A S8-mediated hydrogen-borrowing reduction/cyclization/aromatization strategy for the preparation of indolo[3,2-c]quinolines has been described. The present procedure starts from 2-(2-nitrophenyl)indoles and amines, providing a wide range of indolo[3,2-c]quinolines in 39%–75% yields under metal-free conditions. In this work, nitro-group reduction, CN bond cleavage, CN bond formation, and cyclization are achieved in one pot.
item.dc:creator: Bin Tan, Jiao Liu, Zeng Cheng, Yang Li, Yuxia Li, Guo‐Jun Deng, Shanping Chen
item.dc:title: Metal‐Free Synthesis of Indolo[3,2‐c]quinolines via S8‐Mediated Hydrogen‐Borrowing Reduction/Cyclization/Aromatization Sequence

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Regioselective Alkyne–Aldehyde Coupling under Metal‐Free and Reductant‐Free Conditions
item.dc:description:
Herein, we described a novel strategy for the regioselective alkyne–aldehyde coupling without the use of catalysts and exogenous reductants, which involved a self‐assembly triple cascade reaction of acyl‐stabilized sulfur ylides with two different aldehydes. This multicomponent domino reaction features exclusive chemo‐ and regioselectivity, excellent E/Z selectivity, a broad substrate scope, and excellent functional group compatibility. Moreover, the structurally diverse complex furan‐substituted allylic alcohol products prepared by the multicomponent domino reaction from three simple starting materials showed greater bioactivity against liver fibrosis than pifenidone, a broad‐spectrum treatment for fibrosis, hence showing potential to be used as lead candidates for anti‐liver fibrosis treatments.
Herein, we described a novel strategy for regioselective alkyne–aldehyde coupling without the use of catalysts and exogenous reductants. The coupling involved a self‐assembly triple cascade reaction of acyl‐stabilized sulfur ylides with two different aldehydes. Critical to the success of the alkyne–aldehyde coupling is that acyl‐stabilized sulfur ylides react with substituted propiolaldehydes preferentially to form a key multisubstituted 2,3‐dihydrofurylsulfonium salt intermediate bearing a thionium cation at the homopropargylic position, which can be subsequently captured by arylaldehydes as electrophiles via an allenic anion. This multicomponent domino reaction provides easy access to structurally diverse and densely functionalized furan‐substituted allylic alcohols from three simple starting materials under mild, operationally simple conditions, with exclusive chemo‐ and regioselectivity, excellent E/Z selectivity, a wide substrate scope, and high functional group tolerance. Moreover, the furan‐substituted allylic alcohol products showed greater bioactivity against liver fibrosis than pifenidone, a broad‐spectrum treatment for fibrosis, hence showing potential to be used as lead candidates for anti‐liver fibrosis treatments.
item.content:encoded:

This multicomponent domino reaction features exclusive chemo- and regioselectivity, excellent E/Z selectivity, a broad substrate scope, and excellent functional group compatibility. Moreover, the structurally diverse complex furan-substituted allylic alcohol products prepared by the multicomponent domino reaction from three simple starting materials showed greater bioactivity against liver fibrosis than pifenidone, a broad-spectrum treatment for fibrosis, hence showing potential to be used as lead candidates for anti-liver fibrosis treatments.


Herein, we described a novel strategy for regioselective alkyne–aldehyde coupling without the use of catalysts and exogenous reductants. The coupling involved a self-assembly triple cascade reaction of acyl-stabilized sulfur ylides with two different aldehydes. Critical to the success of the alkyne–aldehyde coupling is that acyl-stabilized sulfur ylides react with substituted propiolaldehydes preferentially to form a key multisubstituted 2,3-dihydrofurylsulfonium salt intermediate bearing a thionium cation at the homopropargylic position, which can be subsequently captured by arylaldehydes as electrophiles via an allenic anion. This multicomponent domino reaction provides easy access to structurally diverse and densely functionalized furan-substituted allylic alcohols from three simple starting materials under mild, operationally simple conditions, with exclusive chemo- and regioselectivity, excellent E/Z selectivity, a wide substrate scope, and high functional group tolerance. Moreover, the furan-substituted allylic alcohol products showed greater bioactivity against liver fibrosis than pifenidone, a broad-spectrum treatment for fibrosis, hence showing potential to be used as lead candidates for anti-liver fibrosis treatments.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Diphenylphosphinic Acid‐Catalyzed and Tf2O‐Promoted Efficient Synthesis of γ‐Pyrones from β‐Keto Ester Compounds
item.dc:description:
We have developed an efficient diphenylphosphinic acid‐catalyzed and Tf2O‐promoted condensation reaction of β‐keto esters. The catalytic amount of diphenylphosphinic acid acts as either a Brønsted acid or a Lewis base in this reaction, which significantly enhances the reaction efficiency, enabling the isolation of a variety of γ‐pyrone products in satisfactory to high yields.
A diphenylphosphinic acid‐catalyzed and Tf2O‐promoted intermolecular condensation of β‐keto ester compounds is developed. The catalytic amount of diphenylphosphinic acid can significantly enhance the reaction efficiency, enabling the isolation of a variety of γ‐pyrone products in good to high yields. Several control experiments provided key insights into the mechanism, revealing the dual role of diphenylphosphinic acid: it acts not only as a Brønsted acid catalyst to accelerate the reaction but also as a Lewis base to capture the alkyl group. Furthermore, this reaction can be successfully scaled up without loss of efficiency, demonstrating its potential for large‐scale industrial applications.
item.content:encoded:
We have developed an efficient diphenylphosphinic acid-catalyzed and Tf2O-promoted condensation reaction of β-keto esters. The catalytic amount of diphenylphosphinic acid acts as either a Brønsted acid or a Lewis base in this reaction, which significantly enhances the reaction efficiency, enabling the isolation of a variety of γ-pyrone products in satisfactory to high yields.


A diphenylphosphinic acid-catalyzed and Tf2O-promoted intermolecular condensation of β-keto ester compounds is developed. The catalytic amount of diphenylphosphinic acid can significantly enhance the reaction efficiency, enabling the isolation of a variety of γ-pyrone products in good to high yields. Several control experiments provided key insights into the mechanism, revealing the dual role of diphenylphosphinic acid: it acts not only as a Brønsted acid catalyst to accelerate the reaction but also as a Lewis base to capture the alkyl group. Furthermore, this reaction can be successfully scaled up without loss of efficiency, demonstrating its potential for large-scale industrial applications.
item.dc:creator: Ming Li, Zhi‐Chao Wang, Zhao‐Kang Wang, Jia‐Yi Wang, Xin Wang, Kai Sun, Bin Yang
item.dc:title: Diphenylphosphinic Acid‐Catalyzed and Tf2O‐Promoted Efficient Synthesis of γ‐Pyrones from β‐Keto Ester Compounds

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Catalytic Enantioselective Synthesis of Conformationally Stable C(sp2)−C(sp3) Naphthocoumarin Atropisomers
item.dc:description:
The enantioselective synthesis of naphthocoumarin adducts via a tandem organocatalytic 1,4‐addition/decarboxylation delivers excellent control over a newly forged stereocenter and yields configurationally stable synclinal atropisomers. The process features broad substrate scope and scalability, and its structural and mechanistic foundations are elucidated through detailed nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), X‐ray, and density functional theory (DFT) analyses.
A straightforward atroposelective access to enantiomerically enriched 2‐hydroxy‐3‐(2‐oxochroman‐4‐yi)naphthalene‐1,4‐diones (with yields ranging from 32% to 87% and enantiomeric excesses up to 99%) is described. Using an organocatalytic approach, 2‐hydroxynaphthoquinone reacts with a 3‐coumarin‐3‐carboxylic acid through a tandem 1,4‐addition/decarboxylation process initiated by a thiourea‐functionalized cinchona alkaloid, which efficiently controls the stereochemistry of a newly forged stereocenter, while simultaneously directing the formation of a configurationally stable C(sp2)−C(sp3) synclinal atropisomer. The methodology has been explored across a broad substrate scope, and the results are supported by detailed nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) analyses, single‐crystal X‐ray diffraction, and density functional theory (DFT) calculations.
item.content:encoded:
The enantioselective synthesis of naphthocoumarin adducts via a tandem organocatalytic 1,4-addition/decarboxylation delivers excellent control over a newly forged stereocenter and yields configurationally stable synclinal atropisomers. The process features broad substrate scope and scalability, and its structural and mechanistic foundations are elucidated through detailed nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), X-ray, and density functional theory (DFT) analyses.


A straightforward atroposelective access to enantiomerically enriched 2-hydroxy-3-(2-oxochroman-4-yi)naphthalene-1,4-diones (with yields ranging from 32% to 87% and enantiomeric excesses up to 99%) is described. Using an organocatalytic approach, 2-hydroxynaphthoquinone reacts with a 3-coumarin-3-carboxylic acid through a tandem 1,4-addition/decarboxylation process initiated by a thiourea-functionalized cinchona alkaloid, which efficiently controls the stereochemistry of a newly forged stereocenter, while simultaneously directing the formation of a configurationally stable C(sp2)−C(sp3) synclinal atropisomer. The methodology has been explored across a broad substrate scope, and the results are supported by detailed nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) analyses, single-crystal X-ray diffraction, and density functional theory (DFT) calculations.
item.dc:creator: M. Chiara Cabua, Davide Moi, Emanuele Cocco, Valentina Onnis, Enrico Podda, David J. Aitken, Armando Carlone, Renzo Luisi, Enrica Tuveri, Drew F. Parsons, Francesco Secci
item.dc:title: Catalytic Enantioselective Synthesis of Conformationally Stable C(sp2)−C(sp3) Naphthocoumarin Atropisomers

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Transition Metal‐Catalyzed Functionalization of Nonallylic Alkenyl Alcohols via Chain‐Walking Strategies
item.dc:description:
This minireview outlines recent advances in chain‐walking reactions of nonallylic alkenyl alcohols catalyzed by various transition metals, including palladium, ruthenium, rhodium, iridium, and nickel. Furthermore, the corresponding reaction mechanisms are discussed.
Transition metal‐catalyzed chain‐walking reactions have emerged as a powerful and valuable strategy for the construction of highly functionalized organic skeletons. While considerable efforts have been focused on transformations of allylic alcohols, remote functionalization of nonallylic alkenyl alcohols is less explored and has attracted great attention in recent years due to the unique mechanistic pathways and ready construction of diverse organic frameworks. This minireview highlights recent advances in chain‐walking reactions of nonallylic alkenyl alcohols, catalyzed by various transition metal species, including palladium, ruthenium, rhodium, iridium, and nickel.
item.content:encoded:

This minireview highlights recent advances in chain-walking reactions of nonallylic alkenyl alcohols, catalyzed by various transition metal species, including palladium, ruthenium, rhodium, iridium, and nickel.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible‐Light‐Promoted Radical Markovnikov Bromohydroxylation of Alkenes With Molecular Oxygen and Dioxane as Hydroxyl Source
item.dc:description: A visible‐light‐promoted radical bromohydroxylation reaction of quinazolinone‐tethered alkenes with CBr4 as a bromine source has been developed, which affords vicinal bromohydrins as products in up to 66% yields. This work represents the first example of employing molecular oxygen and dioxane as hydroxyl source in bromohydroxylation reaction of alkenes. Control experiments and DFT calculations disclose that the reaction proceeds via a Markovnikov regioselectivity. This reaction features mild conditions and scale‐up applicability, which represents a new bromohydroxylation reaction mode and also provides an easy and green access to vicinal bromohydrins.
item.content:encoded: A visible-light-promoted radical bromohydroxylation reaction of quinazolinone-tethered alkenes with CBr4 as a bromine source has been developed, which affords vicinal bromohydrins as products in up to 66% yields. This work represents the first example of employing molecular oxygen and dioxane as hydroxyl source in bromohydroxylation reaction of alkenes. Control experiments and DFT calculations disclose that the reaction proceeds via a Markovnikov regioselectivity. This reaction features mild conditions and scale-up applicability, which represents a new bromohydroxylation reaction mode and also provides an easy and green access to vicinal bromohydrins.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Sono‐Photoinduced Self‐Catalyzed Acyloxylation of 4H‐Pyrido[1,2‐a]Pyrimidin‐4‐Ones with Aryl Diacyl Peroxides and Bioactivities of Products
item.dc:description:
A novel self‐catalyzed sono‐photoinduced C–H acyloxylation of 4H‐pyrido[1,2‐a]pyrimidin‐4‐ones with aryl diacyl peroxides has been established. This protocol employs eco‐friendly DMC as the solvent and proceeds efficiently without exogenous catalysts or additives, affording C3‐acyloxylated derivatives in good to excellent yields.
A novel self‐catalyzed sono‐photoinduced C–H acyloxylation of 4H‐pyrido[1,2‐a]pyrimidin‐4‐ones with aryl diacyl peroxides has been established. This protocol employs eco‐friendly DMC as the solvent and proceeds efficiently without exogenous catalysts or additives, affording C3‐acyloxylated derivatives in good to excellent yields. The synergistic sono‐photocatalysis strategy significantly enhances reaction kinetics, product yields, and energy efficiency compared to traditional photocatalytic methods. Moreover, preliminary biological assays reveal that the synthesized compounds exhibit promising antitumor activities.
item.content:encoded:
A novel self-catalyzed sono-photoinduced C–H acyloxylation of 4H-pyrido[1,2-a]pyrimidin-4-ones with aryl diacyl peroxides has been established. This protocol employs eco-friendly DMC as the solvent and proceeds efficiently without exogenous catalysts or additives, affording C3-acyloxylated derivatives in good to excellent yields.


A novel self-catalyzed sono-photoinduced C–H acyloxylation of 4H-pyrido[1,2-a]pyrimidin-4-ones with aryl diacyl peroxides has been established. This protocol employs eco-friendly DMC as the solvent and proceeds efficiently without exogenous catalysts or additives, affording C3-acyloxylated derivatives in good to excellent yields. The synergistic sono-photocatalysis strategy significantly enhances reaction kinetics, product yields, and energy efficiency compared to traditional photocatalytic methods. Moreover, preliminary biological assays reveal that the synthesized compounds exhibit promising antitumor activities.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Substrate‐Enabled Base‐Promoted Regioselective (3+2) Annulation of 1,4‐Enynoates with N‐Imine(iso)quinoline Ylides: One‐Pot Access to Pyrazolo(iso)quinoline Derivatives
item.dc:description:
Substrate‐enabled base‐promoted regioselective (3 + 2) annulation of enynes with N‐Imine(iso)quinoline ylides: One‐pot access to pyrazolo(iso)quinoline derivatives.
Herein, we describe a base‐promoted successive (3 + 2) annulation/aromatization reaction of hex‐5‐en‐2‐ynoates with (iso)quinolin‐2‐ium‐2‐yl(tosyl)amides, in which hex‐5‐en‐2‐ynoates act as a novel and regioselectively distinct C2 synthon in this process. The use of β‐ and γ‐carbons of the substrates for CC bond formation is distinct from the substrate‐controlled divergent reactivity reported previously. The reaction provides an efficient route to highly functionalized pyrazolo[5,1‐a](iso)quinolines, delivering moderate to good yields with exclusive regioselectivity under mild conditions. The products can be further functionalized through reduction or hydrogenation, indicating their potential applications.
item.content:encoded:
Substrate-enabled base-promoted regioselective (3 + 2) annulation of enynes with N-Imine(iso)quinoline ylides: One-pot access to pyrazolo(iso)quinoline derivatives.


Herein, we describe a base-promoted successive (3 + 2) annulation/aromatization reaction of hex-5-en-2-ynoates with (iso)quinolin-2-ium-2-yl(tosyl)amides, in which hex-5-en-2-ynoates act as a novel and regioselectively distinct C2 synthon in this process. The use of β- and γ-carbons of the substrates for CC bond formation is distinct from the substrate-controlled divergent reactivity reported previously. The reaction provides an efficient route to highly functionalized pyrazolo[5,1-a](iso)quinolines, delivering moderate to good yields with exclusive regioselectivity under mild conditions. The products can be further functionalized through reduction or hydrogenation, indicating their potential applications.
item.dc:creator: Mengyue Zhang, Yutao Duan, Jianghong Tan, Yuanyuan Chang, Zhongjun Li, Er‐Qing Li
item.dc:title: Substrate‐Enabled Base‐Promoted Regioselective (3+2) Annulation of 1,4‐Enynoates with N‐Imine(iso)quinoline Ylides: One‐Pot Access to Pyrazolo(iso)quinoline Derivatives

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: A Sterically Congested but Reactive Diboron Reagent: Synthesis of [OxB]2 and Direct Access to Stable Alkylboron Building Blocks
item.dc:description:
Steric shielding over electronic deactivation: a congested diboron reagent ([OxB]2) defies expectations, enabling direct catalytic synthesis of stable, deprotectable alkylborons.
This article reports the synthesis of the dimer of oxazaborolidinone (OxB), [OxB]2, a sterically hindered protecting group for boron. This highly congested unique diboron reagent functions as an efficient borylation agent, enabling the direct synthesis of alkylboron compounds, which has been challenging with conventional methods. Remarkably, despite its significant steric bulk, [OxB]2 undergoes smooth transmetalation with a copper catalyst. Through complementary reductive borylation and cross‐coupling with alkyl halides, we successfully prepared a range of primary and secondary alkylboron compounds—traditionally unstable and difficult to access—as stable Alkyl‐OxB derivatives. Furthermore, the resulting Alkyl‐OxB derivatives can be readily deprotected under mild conditions to furnish the corresponding alkylboronic acids. Leveraging this property, we demonstrated the iterative Suzuki–Miyaura cross‐coupling of protected alkylboron species, a transformation that has remained elusive to date.
item.content:encoded:
Steric shielding over electronic deactivation: a congested diboron reagent ([OxB]2) defies expectations, enabling direct catalytic synthesis of stable, deprotectable alkylborons.


This article reports the synthesis of the dimer of oxazaborolidinone (OxB), [OxB]2, a sterically hindered protecting group for boron. This highly congested unique diboron reagent functions as an efficient borylation agent, enabling the direct synthesis of alkylboron compounds, which has been challenging with conventional methods. Remarkably, despite its significant steric bulk, [OxB]2 undergoes smooth transmetalation with a copper catalyst. Through complementary reductive borylation and cross-coupling with alkyl halides, we successfully prepared a range of primary and secondary alkylboron compounds—traditionally unstable and difficult to access—as stable Alkyl-OxB derivatives. Furthermore, the resulting Alkyl-OxB derivatives can be readily deprotected under mild conditions to furnish the corresponding alkylboronic acids. Leveraging this property, we demonstrated the iterative Suzuki–Miyaura cross-coupling of protected alkylboron species, a transformation that has remained elusive to date.

item.dc:title: A Sterically Congested but Reactive Diboron Reagent: Synthesis of [OxB]2 and Direct Access to Stable Alkylboron Building Blocks

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper Catalyzed Three‐Component Trifluoromethylthio‐Sulfonylation of Allenes
item.dc:description:
A copper‐catalyzed three‐component trifluoromethylthio‐sulfonylation of allenes has been developed. A variety of CF3S‐containing vinyl sulfones are obtained with excellent chemo‐, regio‐, and stereoselectivity under mild conditions. This synergistic strategy exhibits wide functional group tolerance, good broad compatibility with various allenes and sulfonyl chlorides, and shows potential for applications in synthetic chemistry.
A new three‐component trifluoromethylthio‐sulfonylation of allenes via copper catalysis has been established, affording a variety of CF3S‐containing vinyl sulfones with excellent chemo‐, regio‐, and stereoselectivity under mild conditions. This protocol exhibits wide functional group tolerance, good broad compatibility with various allenes and sulfonyl chlorides, and shows potential for applications in synthetic chemistry.

A variety of CF3S-containing vinyl sulfones are obtained with excellent chemo-, regio-, and stereoselectivity under mild conditions. This synergistic strategy exhibits wide functional group tolerance, good broad compatibility with various allenes and sulfonyl chlorides, and shows potential for applications in synthetic chemistry.

This protocol exhibits wide functional group tolerance, good broad compatibility with various allenes and sulfonyl chlorides, and shows potential for applications in synthetic chemistry.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Base‐Controlled Stereodivergent Synthesis of Pyrrolidines Containing An Enaminone Moiety via Regioselective Formal (3 + 2) Cycloaddition Reactions†
item.dc:description:
An efficient base‐controlled stereodivergent synthesis of polysubstituted pyrrolidines has been achieved through a regioselective (3 + 2) cycloaddition/isomerization strategy, employing aminoalkyl α,β‐unsaturated esters and acyclic conjugated imines as reactants. This method enables the convenient interconversion between Z‐ and E‐stereoisomers by simply varying the type of organic or inorganic base, while also demonstrating excellent diastereo‐ and regioselectivity.
An effective stereodivergent synthesis that provides access to both Z‐ and E‐stereoisomers is highly attractive in the fields of organic synthesis and medicinal chemistry. Herein, we demonstrate that base‐controlled stereodivergence allows for the synthesis of a series of cis−2,4‐disubstituted pyrrolidines featuring an exocyclic double bond via a formal (3 + 2) cycloaddition/isomerization strategy, starting from aminoalkyl α,β‐unsaturated esters and acyclic conjugated imines in an environmentally benign manner. This approach not only allows for the facile switching between Z‐ and E‐stereoisomers by varying the type of organic or inorganic base but also facilitates the production of pyrrolidines bearing two tertiary chiral centers with high yields (up to 97%) and excellent chemo‐, regio‐, and diastereoselectivities (in all cases, > 25:1 dr). Additionally, the synthetic utility of this method is further demonstrated via scale‐up reactions and synthetic transformations of the products. Furthermore, molecular docking studies have indicated that the de novo‐synthesized pyrrolidines exhibit potential antibacterial activity.
item.content:encoded:
An efficient base-controlled stereodivergent synthesis of polysubstituted pyrrolidines has been achieved through a regioselective (3 + 2) cycloaddition/isomerization strategy, employing aminoalkyl α,β-unsaturated esters and acyclic conjugated imines as reactants. This method enables the convenient interconversion between Z- and E-stereoisomers by simply varying the type of organic or inorganic base, while also demonstrating excellent diastereo- and regioselectivity.


An effective stereodivergent synthesis that provides access to both Z- and E-stereoisomers is highly attractive in the fields of organic synthesis and medicinal chemistry. Herein, we demonstrate that base-controlled stereodivergence allows for the synthesis of a series of cis−2,4-disubstituted pyrrolidines featuring an exocyclic double bond via a formal (3 + 2) cycloaddition/isomerization strategy, starting from aminoalkyl α,β-unsaturated esters and acyclic conjugated imines in an environmentally benign manner. This approach not only allows for the facile switching between Z- and E-stereoisomers by varying the type of organic or inorganic base but also facilitates the production of pyrrolidines bearing two tertiary chiral centers with high yields (up to 97%) and excellent chemo-, regio-, and diastereoselectivities (in all cases, > 25:1 dr). Additionally, the synthetic utility of this method is further demonstrated via scale-up reactions and synthetic transformations of the products. Furthermore, molecular docking studies have indicated that the de novo-synthesized pyrrolidines exhibit potential antibacterial activity.
item.dc:creator: Kai‐Kai Wang, Na‐Na Zhao, Xiao‐Ran Wang, Bo‐Wen Zhang, Si‐Qi Wei, Yi‐Fan Zhang, Aili Sun, Rongxiang Chen
item.dc:title: Base‐Controlled Stereodivergent Synthesis of Pyrrolidines Containing An Enaminone Moiety via Regioselective Formal (3 + 2) Cycloaddition Reactions†

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Porphyrinic Carbon Microspheres With Surface Co‐(Pyrrolic N)4 Sites for Efficient Hydrogen Peroxide Electrosynthesis
item.dc:description:
A 3D multidirectional porphyrin polymer‐derivation strategy for precisely engineering Co‐(Pyrrolic N)4 sites on a carbon surface was developed, which enabled efficient and durable electrochemical H2O2 synthesis in 0.1 M perchloric acid, 0.1 M phosphate‐buffered saline, and 0.1 M potassium hydroxide, with the highest H2O2 production rate of 3.12 mol·g−1·h−1 in an alkaline flow cell with about 90% Faradaic efficiency.
A 3D multidirectional covalent hexaphenylbenzene‐based porphyrin polymer with precasting Co‐(Pyrrolic N)4 moieties was thermally transformed into carbon microspheres with surface‐exposed Co‐(Pyrrolic N)4 sites. The resulting material demonstrated efficient electrocatalytic performance for the two‐electron oxygen reduction reaction (2e‐ ORR) to hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in 0.1 M perchloric acid (HClO4, pH = 1), 0.1 M phosphate‐buffered saline (pH = 7), and 0.1 M potassium hydroxide (KOH, pH = 13). In H‐type electrolytic cells, it achieved an H2O2 production rate of about 512–850 mmol gcat.−1 h−1 in three electrolytes, exhibiting better durability. Moreover, the H2O2 production rate was further elevated to 3.12 mol gcat.−1 h−1 in an alkaline flow cell with about 95% Faradaic efficiency. Experimental and theoretical studies revealed that a 3D multidirectional porphyrin polymer facilitated the formation of abundant Co‐(Pyrrolic N)4 sites on the carbon surface, which can effectively adsorb *O2 and *OOH intermediates, thereby promoting a four‐step 2e‐ ORR mechanism with a low energy barrier. This work presents a multidirectional porphyrin polymer‐derivation strategy for precisely engineering Co‐(Pyrrolic N)4 sites on a carbon surface, enabling efficient and durable electrochemical H2O2 synthesis in different pH solutions.
item.content:encoded:
A 3D multidirectional porphyrin polymer-derivation strategy for precisely engineering Co-(Pyrrolic N)4 sites on a carbon surface was developed, which enabled efficient and durable electrochemical H2O2 synthesis in 0.1 M perchloric acid, 0.1 M phosphate-buffered saline, and 0.1 M potassium hydroxide, with the highest H2O2 production rate of 3.12 mol·g−1·h−1 in an alkaline flow cell with about 90% Faradaic efficiency.


A 3D multidirectional covalent hexaphenylbenzene-based porphyrin polymer with precasting Co-(Pyrrolic N)4 moieties was thermally transformed into carbon microspheres with surface-exposed Co-(Pyrrolic N)4 sites. The resulting material demonstrated efficient electrocatalytic performance for the two-electron oxygen reduction reaction (2e- ORR) to hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in 0.1 M perchloric acid (HClO4, pH = 1), 0.1 M phosphate-buffered saline (pH = 7), and 0.1 M potassium hydroxide (KOH, pH = 13). In H-type electrolytic cells, it achieved an H2O2 production rate of about 512–850 mmol gcat. −1 h−1 in three electrolytes, exhibiting better durability. Moreover, the H2O2 production rate was further elevated to 3.12 mol gcat. −1 h−1 in an alkaline flow cell with about 95% Faradaic efficiency. Experimental and theoretical studies revealed that a 3D multidirectional porphyrin polymer facilitated the formation of abundant Co-(Pyrrolic N)4 sites on the carbon surface, which can effectively adsorb *O2 and *OOH intermediates, thereby promoting a four-step 2e- ORR mechanism with a low energy barrier. This work presents a multidirectional porphyrin polymer-derivation strategy for precisely engineering Co-(Pyrrolic N)4 sites on a carbon surface, enabling efficient and durable electrochemical H2O2 synthesis in different pH solutions.
item.dc:creator: Fuping Chen, Xueqin Peng, Aiguo Kong
item.dc:title: Porphyrinic Carbon Microspheres With Surface Co‐(Pyrrolic N)4 Sites for Efficient Hydrogen Peroxide Electrosynthesis

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Carbon Skeleton and Oxygen Vacancy Enhanced Z‐Scheme TiO2/C‐CN Heterojunction for Selective Lactic Acid Production
item.dc:description:
A Z‐scheme TiO2/C‐CN photocatalyst modified by carbon skeleton and oxygen vacancy has been constructed. It synergistically enhances light absorption, charge separation, and O2 adsorption/activation to facilitate effective ·O−2 generation. This selectively cleaves the C3C4 bond of glucose intermediates to efficiently produce lactic acid (92.3% yield and 96.4% selectivity), providing a rational strategy for biomass valorization.
The photocatalytic conversion of biomass‐derived monosaccharides to lactic acid (LA) provides a sustainable approach for the high‐value utilization of biomass. However, conventional photocatalysts suffer from insufficient visible light absorption, rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, and inadequate surface active sites, which severely hinder the generation of key active species, thereby limiting the catalytic activity and LA selectivity. Herein, this study constructs a Z‐scheme heterojunction photocatalyst TiO2/C‐CN by combining carbon skeleton and oxygen vacancy (VO)–modified TiO2 with graphitic carbon nitride (CN), with excellent photocatalytic performance. Under visible light irradiation, it achieves 92.3% LA yield and 96.4% selectivity within 60 min. The carbon skeleton effectively enhances visible light absorption and electron transport, VO provides abundant active sites for the adsorption and activation of oxygen, and the construction of the Z‐scheme heterojunction achieves efficient charge separation while retaining strong redox capability. The synergistic effect of these three components facilitates the efficient generation of superoxide radicals (·O2−$^{-1}$), which selectively cleave the C3C4 bond of glucose intermediates, directing the reaction toward LA formation. This study provides a rational strategy to design efficient and precise photocatalysts for biomass valorization through the synergistic regulation of electron transport channels, defect sites, and heterojunction engineering.
item.content:encoded:
A Z-scheme TiO2/C-CN photocatalyst modified by carbon skeleton and oxygen vacancy has been constructed. It synergistically enhances light absorption, charge separation, and O2 adsorption/activation to facilitate effective ·O− 2 generation. This selectively cleaves the C3C4 bond of glucose intermediates to efficiently produce lactic acid (92.3% yield and 96.4% selectivity), providing a rational strategy for biomass valorization.


The photocatalytic conversion of biomass-derived monosaccharides to lactic acid (LA) provides a sustainable approach for the high-value utilization of biomass. However, conventional photocatalysts suffer from insufficient visible light absorption, rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, and inadequate surface active sites, which severely hinder the generation of key active species, thereby limiting the catalytic activity and LA selectivity. Herein, this study constructs a Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst TiO2/C-CN by combining carbon skeleton and oxygen vacancy (VO)–modified TiO2 with graphitic carbon nitride (CN), with excellent photocatalytic performance. Under visible light irradiation, it achieves 92.3% LA yield and 96.4% selectivity within 60 min. The carbon skeleton effectively enhances visible light absorption and electron transport, VO provides abundant active sites for the adsorption and activation of oxygen, and the construction of the Z-scheme heterojunction achieves efficient charge separation while retaining strong redox capability. The synergistic effect of these three components facilitates the efficient generation of superoxide radicals (·O2−$^{-1}$), which selectively cleave the C3C4 bond of glucose intermediates, directing the reaction toward LA formation. This study provides a rational strategy to design efficient and precise photocatalysts for biomass valorization through the synergistic regulation of electron transport channels, defect sites, and heterojunction engineering.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Mechanochemical Dehalogenative Deuteration of Alkyl Bromides via Alkylmanganese Reagents
item.dc:description:
We herein present a mechanochemical strategy for the efficient synthesis of alkylmanganese nucleophiles via direct activation of alkyl bromides with elemental manganese, which facilitates a streamlined one‐pot dehalogenative deuteration process employing CD3OD as the deuterium source.
We herein report a mechanochemical protocol for the synthesis of alkylmanganese reagents via the direct reaction of alkyl bromides with manganese metal, followed by one‐pot deuteration. This solvent‐less and additive‐free methodology facilitates efficient dehalogenative deuteration under ambient conditions within 1 h, establishing a practical paradigm for alkylmanganese reagent synthesis and offering a versatile approach for dehalogenative deuteration.
item.content:encoded:
We herein present a mechanochemical strategy for the efficient synthesis of alkylmanganese nucleophiles via direct activation of alkyl bromides with elemental manganese, which facilitates a streamlined one-pot dehalogenative deuteration process employing CD3OD as the deuterium source.


We herein report a mechanochemical protocol for the synthesis of alkylmanganese reagents via the direct reaction of alkyl bromides with manganese metal, followed by one-pot deuteration. This solvent-less and additive-free methodology facilitates efficient dehalogenative deuteration under ambient conditions within 1 h, establishing a practical paradigm for alkylmanganese reagent synthesis and offering a versatile approach for dehalogenative deuteration.

item.dc:title: Mechanochemical Dehalogenative Deuteration of Alkyl Bromides via Alkylmanganese Reagents

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Deconstructive Alkynylation of Spiro‐Dihydroquinazolinones via Aromatization‐Driven Ring Opening Under Visible Light Irradiation
item.dc:description:
Deconstructive alkynylation of spiro‐dihydroquinazolinones via aromatization‐driven ring opening under visible light irradiation.
Here, we report a visible light‐driven, metal‐free photoredox strategy enabling the deconstructive C(sp3)–C(sp) coupling of spiro‐dihydroquinazolinones with alkynyl sulfones, providing efficient access to structurally diverse alkynylated quinazolinone scaffolds via an aromatization‐induced radical pathway under mild conditions. Furthermore, translation of the batch protocol to a PFA‐based continuous‐flow reactor enhances reaction efficiency, shortens reaction time, and demonstrates its practical scalability. Mechanistic studies supports the involvement of a radical mediated transformation.
item.content:encoded:
Deconstructive alkynylation of spiro-dihydroquinazolinones via aromatization-driven ring opening under visible light irradiation.


Here, we report a visible light-driven, metal-free photoredox strategy enabling the deconstructive C(sp3)–C(sp) coupling of spiro-dihydroquinazolinones with alkynyl sulfones, providing efficient access to structurally diverse alkynylated quinazolinone scaffolds via an aromatization-induced radical pathway under mild conditions. Furthermore, translation of the batch protocol to a PFA-based continuous-flow reactor enhances reaction efficiency, shortens reaction time, and demonstrates its practical scalability. Mechanistic studies supports the involvement of a radical mediated transformation.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: One‐Pot Biocatalytic Synthesis of D‐Amino Acids via a Deracemization Cascade Exploiting Amine Transaminase as a Thermodynamic Sink
item.dc:description:
A whole‐cell deracemization cascade combining L‐amino acid deaminase (LAAD), D‐amino acid transaminase (DAAT), and R‐selective amine transaminase (RATA) enables efficient D‐amino acid synthesis. RATA‐driven D‐alanine recycling, using α‐methylbenzylamine as a cosubstrate, serves as a robust equilibrium shifter that overcomes the thermodynamic limitation of DAAT reactions and suppresses metabolic pyruvate depletion. The modular catalytic construct allows independent activity tuning of each module.
Thermodynamically neutral equilibria often limit the synthetic utility of amino acid transaminases. An effective strategy to overcome this limitation is to design a catalytic cascade that circumvents the thermodynamic barrier by recycling the coproduct into the cosubstrate. Here, we present a whole‐cell deracemization cascade for the synthesis of D‐amino acids, in which the D‐amino acid transaminase (DAAT) reaction is thermodynamically driven by an R‐selective amine transaminase (RATA). The cascade consists of three compartmentalized modules, each implemented in a distinct Escherichia coli whole‐cell biocatalyst: (i) an acceptor‐supply module (ASM) that converts the L‐enantiomer of racemic amino acid to α‐keto acid using L‐amino acid deaminase (LAAD); (ii) an amine‐transfer module (ATM) that produces D‐amino acid from the α‐keto acid using DAAT with D‐alanine; and (iii) a donor‐recycling module (DRM) that regenerates D‐alanine using RATA with α‐methylbenzylamine (α‐MBA). Integration of the three modules enabled one‐pot deracemization of amino acids within the overlapping substrate scope of LAAD and DAAT, affording the desired D‐amino acids in yields exceeding 90%. This study demonstrates a whole‐cell cascade system that leverages highly exergonic α‐MBA‐mediated conversion of pyruvate to D‐alanine to overcome the unfavorable DAAT equilibrium, providing an efficient route for synthesizing D‐amino acids from inexpensive racemic precursors.
item.content:encoded:
A whole-cell deracemization cascade combining L-amino acid deaminase (LAAD), D-amino acid transaminase (DAAT), and R-selective amine transaminase (RATA) enables efficient D-amino acid synthesis. RATA-driven D-alanine recycling, using α-methylbenzylamine as a cosubstrate, serves as a robust equilibrium shifter that overcomes the thermodynamic limitation of DAAT reactions and suppresses metabolic pyruvate depletion. The modular catalytic construct allows independent activity tuning of each module.


Thermodynamically neutral equilibria often limit the synthetic utility of amino acid transaminases. An effective strategy to overcome this limitation is to design a catalytic cascade that circumvents the thermodynamic barrier by recycling the coproduct into the cosubstrate. Here, we present a whole-cell deracemization cascade for the synthesis of D-amino acids, in which the D-amino acid transaminase (DAAT) reaction is thermodynamically driven by an R-selective amine transaminase (RATA). The cascade consists of three compartmentalized modules, each implemented in a distinct Escherichia coli whole-cell biocatalyst: (i) an acceptor-supply module (ASM) that converts the L-enantiomer of racemic amino acid to α-keto acid using L-amino acid deaminase (LAAD); (ii) an amine-transfer module (ATM) that produces D-amino acid from the α-keto acid using DAAT with D-alanine; and (iii) a donor-recycling module (DRM) that regenerates D-alanine using RATA with α-methylbenzylamine (α-MBA). Integration of the three modules enabled one-pot deracemization of amino acids within the overlapping substrate scope of LAAD and DAAT, affording the desired D-amino acids in yields exceeding 90%. This study demonstrates a whole-cell cascade system that leverages highly exergonic α-MBA-mediated conversion of pyruvate to D-alanine to overcome the unfavorable DAAT equilibrium, providing an efficient route for synthesizing D-amino acids from inexpensive racemic precursors.

item.dc:title: One‐Pot Biocatalytic Synthesis of D‐Amino Acids via a Deracemization Cascade Exploiting Amine Transaminase as a Thermodynamic Sink

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Desymmetrization of Pseudo‐para Diformyl[2.2]Paracyclophane via Brønsted Acid‐Catalyzed Reductive Amination
item.dc:description:
A highly stereoselective desymmetrization of pseudo‐para diformyl[2.2]paracyclophanes is developed via chiral Brønsted acid‐catalyzed reductive amination. This protocol provides efficient access to enantiopure planar chiral paracyclophanes with broad substrate scope, excellent enantioselectivity, and versatile postsynthetic functionalization.
Access to planar chiral molecules remains a longstanding challenge in asymmetric synthesis. Enantiopure [2.2]paracyclophanes are particularly attractive due to their broad applications in medicinal chemistry, asymmetric catalysis, and materials science. Herein, we present a highly stereoselective approach for the desymmetrization of prochiral pseudo‐para diformyl[2.2]paracyclophanes, enabled by chiral Brønsted acid‐catalyzed reductive amination. This method furnishes planar chiral structures with excellent enantiocontrol and broad substrate scope. Moreover, the resulting products can be readily modified, underscoring the utility of this approach as a versatile platform for the asymmetric synthesis of planar chiral [2.2]paracyclophane derivatives.
item.content:encoded:

This protocol provides efficient access to enantiopure planar chiral paracyclophanes with broad substrate scope, excellent enantioselectivity, and versatile postsynthetic functionalization.


Access to planar chiral molecules remains a longstanding challenge in asymmetric synthesis. Enantiopure [2.2]paracyclophanes are particularly attractive due to their broad applications in medicinal chemistry, asymmetric catalysis, and materials science. Herein, we present a highly stereoselective approach for the desymmetrization of prochiral pseudo-para diformyl[2.2]paracyclophanes, enabled by chiral Brønsted acid-catalyzed reductive amination. This method furnishes planar chiral structures with excellent enantiocontrol and broad substrate scope. Moreover, the resulting products can be readily modified, underscoring the utility of this approach as a versatile platform for the asymmetric synthesis of planar chiral [2.2]paracyclophane derivatives.
